Three types of conflict response instincts survive the caveman days. Three types. Fight, flight, and make friends. When cavemen encounter danger, number one, they fought it, number two, they ran from it, or number three, they made friends with it. Others have described this instinctive process as can I eat it, will it eat me, or can I mate with it? You get the idea. The Black Swan Group has derived these ideas from a combination of places. What we've learned from the Harvard program on negotiation, who uses the Thomas Kilman conflict mode instrument, or the TKI assessment tool. Some basic psychology and of course field tested experience. The TKI separates people in five types and then Harvard cuts it down to three types. What they and us believe to be the instinctive types. What we and Harvard drop out of the TKI are the collaborative and cooperative types. We agree that those two types are learned combinations of the three basic instinctive types. You'll hear what those three types are in a moment. Based on our experience at the Black Swan Group, we further advanced these ideas to what you'll see here. For another look at these three types, we recommend Robert Mnookin's book Beyond Winning. While that book is written primarily for lawyers, the second chapter entitled the tension between empathy and assertiveness takes a good look at the three types with only slightly different terminology. And it's a phenomenal chapter on empathy as well. The Black Swan Group named these three types as analysts, accommodators, and assertives. The Hollywood stereotype that the assertive style is the best for negotiation is false. So forget it. Each of us defaults to one of these three instinctive types. It's our basic initial response to threat or conflict. As we grow and learn, many of us at varying rates of speed learn to adapt. Some learn quickly. Some may seem to almost never evolve. This format is the beginning of the discussion, not the end all be all. We're not putting people into a box. However, we've got plenty of experience that indicates the entire world, regardless of gender or ethnicity, splits pretty evenly into these three types. Each style has its pros and cons. Great negotiators will draw upon the complementary attributes from all three types in order to truly master the dealmaking process. Your conflict type tends to show itself during negotiation and bargaining situations. There are critical dynamics during negotiation that each of the three types tend to see in vastly different ways and place vastly different values on. Resiprosity, silence, and the value of relationships are just a few of these things. It's been the experience of the Black Swan Group that impasses in negotiation are most often a result of type mismatch. Another key consideration to the types is that each tends to place an extremely high value on something other than the deal. Each has a deep underlying need that if fulfilled, make them much more likely to agree to your deal. I said earlier that the world splits pretty evenly into the three types. The hidden message here is that two out of the three people you deal with will not be your type. Two out of three times you will be involved in a negotiation and it will be a type mismatch from the beginning. And two out of three times there will be an underlying motivation that is different from yours. And as it turns out is actually more important to them than getting their way. So what does this also mean? Well, simply put, this also means there's no such thing as normal. When they're reacting in ways that make no sense to you, your instant assessment is that they are the ones who are abnormal because after all, you're normal, right? On the other hand, if you can recognize and identify your own negotiating type and that of your counterpart, you can adjust your mindset and strategies accordingly. There is one basic truth about a successful bargaining style. To be good, you should learn to be yourself and know your strengths. For you to be great, you must add to your strengths and you have to find the complementary skills that enhance those strengths. Many times, the strength that got you into a situation is not the strength to get you beyond that point. As the saying goes, what got you here will not get you there. The rest of this chapter will be a quick guide on how you can classify the three types and the best tactics for you to use with them. Click on any of the three types below to learn more about them.
